questions.question: What does the X (bit flip) error do to the state |+⟩ = (|0⟩+|1⟩)/√2?
questions.explanation: X|+⟩ = X(|0⟩+|1⟩)/√2 = (|1⟩+|0⟩)/√2 = |+⟩. The state |+⟩ is an eigenstate of X with eigenvalue +1, so it's unchanged by bit flip errors.

questions.question: What is the physical cause of phase flip (Z) errors?
questions.explanation: Phase flip errors primarily come from dephasing (T2 processes), where the environment causes random phase accumulation, destroying quantum coherence without changing populations.

questions.question: How is the Y error related to X and Z errors?
questions.explanation: Y = iXZ, meaning a Y error is equivalent to applying both a bit flip and a phase flip. This relationship is fundamental to understanding how the Pauli matrices form a basis.

questions.question: Why can correcting just I, X, Y, Z errors handle arbitrary continuous errors?

options.text: Continuous errors don't occur in practice
Syndrome measurement collapses continuous errors to discrete Pauli errors
The Pauli matrices are the only possible errors

questions.explanation: Any error can be written as a superposition of Pauli errors. When we measure the syndrome, the state collapses to one of the discrete Pauli error cases, which we can then correct. This discretization is a key quantum effect.

questions.question: What is the depolarizing channel?

options.text: A channel that only causes bit flips
A channel that applies random Pauli errors (X, Y, or Z) with equal probability
A channel that only affects measurement

questions.explanation: The depolarizing channel applies X, Y, or Z errors each with probability p/3 (and no error with probability 1-p). It models uniform random errors on a qubit.

questions.question: What effect does Z error have on measurement in the computational (Z) basis?

options.text: It has no effect on computational basis measurement probabilities
It causes the qubit to always measure as |0⟩

questions.explanation: Z|ψ⟩ = Z(α|0⟩ + β|1⟩) = α|0⟩ - β|1⟩. The probabilities |α|² and |β|² are unchanged, so Z-basis measurement gives the same results. However, X-basis measurements would be completely different.

questions.question: Phase flip errors have a direct classical analog, just like bit flip errors.
questions.explanation: False. Classical bits don't have phase, so there's no classical analog to phase flip errors. This is a uniquely quantum error type that affects the relative phase between superposition components.
